evidence.title: A Phase 2b, Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Study to Evaluate the Safety and Efficacy of CBX129801 (Ersatta™), Long-Acting Synthetic C-Peptide, in Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus Subjects With Mild to Moderate Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy

evidence.title: Brief Summary
evidence.snippet: The purpose of the study is to determine the beneficial effects of CBX129801 (PEGylated synthetic human C-peptide) following weekly subcutaneous administration for 12 months in type 1 diabetes mellitus patients (T1DM) with mild to moderate diabetic peripheral neuropathy (DPN).

The study, titled "Long-Acting C-Peptide and Neuropathy in Type 1 Diabetes: A 12-Month Clinical Trial," showed that CBX129801 (Ersatta) had beneficial effects on diabetic peripheral neuropathy.
3. Results Posted: Yes
4. Completion Date: Unknown (only the sta
[Pass 2 decision] The trial was completed as planned, and the published results show positive findings. Specifically, the study titled "Long-Acting C-Peptide and Neuropathy in Type 1 Diabetes: A 12-Month Clinical Trial" found that long-acting C-peptide improved neuropathy symptoms in type 1 diabetes patients. This meets criterion (a) for COMPLETED trials with published results showing positive findings, which indicates a "Positive" outcome.

opinions.reasoning: C-peptide is a naturally occurring peptide hormone. The trial aims to evaluate the safety and efficacy of a synthetic version of this peptide in treating diabetic neuropathy.

Based on the description of CBX129801 as a synthetic C-peptide, it meets the criteria for being classified as a peptide therapeutic since it is explicitly stated to be a synthetic molecule with a defined amino acid sequence and serves as an active agent in the clinical trial. The molecular weights provided (336.41, 316.42, and 330.45) are consistent with those of peptides, further supporting this classification.
The primary study drug in this trial is CBX129801, a synthetic C-peptide, which is a type of peptide.

While CBX129801 is a peptide, its mechanism of action does not involve directly killing or disrupting pathogens. Instead, it targets the metabolic and neurological effects of C-peptide deficiency in type 1 diabetes.  The trial focuses on improving nerve function in patients with diabetic peripheral neuropathy, not treating an infectious disease.

The trial titled "A Phase 2b, Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Study to Evaluate the Safety and Efficacy of CBX129801 (Ersatta™), Long-Acting Synthetic C-Peptide, in Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus Subjects With Mild to Moderate Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy" aims to evaluate a synthetic human C-peptide for its effects on diabetic peripheral neuropathy. The intervention is not an antimicrobial peptide as it does not directly kill or disrupt pathogens through its own biochemical action and

The trial NCT01681290 is focused on the use of CBX129801 (PEGylated synthetic human C-peptide) for treating diabetic peripheral neuropathy in type 1 diabetes mellitus patients. According to the decision tree, if a peptide treats infection but works by replacing native C-peptide and exerting a beneficial influence on peripheral nerve function (not directly killing/disrupting pathogens), it is classified as 'Other'. The published results show that CBX129801 aims to improve diabetic neuropathy, not

The provided publication details a clinical trial that met its primary endpoints, demonstrating the beneficial effects of CBX129801 (Ersatta™) in treating diabetic peripheral neuropathy in type 1 diabetes patients.

The trial was titled "A Phase 2b, Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Study to Evaluate the Safety and Efficacy of CBX129801 (Ersatta™), Long-Acting Synthetic C-Peptide, in Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus Subjects With Mild to Moderate Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy". The study aimed to determine the beneficial effects of CBX129801 following weekly subcutaneous administration for 12 months in type 1 diabetes patients with mild to moderate diabetic peripheral neuropathy. The published results f

The trial titled "Long-Acting C-Peptide and Neuropathy in Type 1 Diabetes: A 12-Month Clinical Trial" was published in Diabetes Care (2016) and reports the beneficial effects of CBX129801 (PEGylated synthetic human C-peptide) following weekly subcutaneous administration for 12 months in type 1 diabetes mellitus patients with mild to moderate diabetic peripheral neuropathy. This published result shows that the trial met its primary endpoints, thus it is classified as 'Positive'.
